definition: The 1947-1951 financial repression period offers structural parallels to today (debt/GDP ~110% vs ~125%, elevated CB balance sheet, policy framework transition). Equity returns were strong (~10-15% annualized nominal) despite bond losses, consistent with the Kalecki-Levy mechanism. However, the critical difference — 1947-51 moved toward fiscal surplus while today moves toward larger deficits — limits the analogue's utility for predicting resolution.
evidence: Elevated CB balance sheet in both periods
Policy framework transition in both periods
Strong equity returns (~10-15% nominal annualized) despite bond losses in 1947-51
open_questions: Does the opposite fiscal trajectory (surplus then vs deficit now) fundamentally invalidate the analogue?
Can the Kalecki-Levy mechanism support equity returns during financial repression under current fiscal structure?
Is the analogue useful for real rate path and equity-vs-bond relative performance even if regime duration is unpredictable?
